sentences.base_language: I just got some great news!
sentences.romanized: Wǒ gāng shōudào yī gè hǎo xiāoxi!
sentences.grammarNote:
**刚** (gāng) = 'just (did something a moment ago)'. It implies very recent completion. **收到** = 'received' (**收** = receive, **到** = resultative complement indicating success).
**消息** (xiāoxi) = news/information. Measure word: **一个消息** (a piece of news) or **一条消息** (also used for news/messages).

sentences.romanized: Wǒ dédào nà fèn gōngzuò le!
sentences.grammarNote:
**得到** (dédào) = 'to obtain/get'. **了** at the end of the sentence marks a change of state — something has now become true that wasn't before. It's the 'new situation' **了**.
**份** (fèn) is the measure word for jobs, portions, documents, sets: **一份工作** (a job), **一份礼物** (a gift).

sentences.base_language: I'm so happy I could cry.
sentences.grammarNote:
**得** (de) as a degree complement: adjective + **得** + result/degree. '**高兴得想哭**' = 'so happy (that I) want to cry'. This **得** is different from **的** (possessive/modifier) and **地** (adverbial). Tone and character differ!
Three different **de** particles:
• **的** — noun modifier: 好的 (good one)
• **地** — adverb marker: 高兴地说 (say happily)
• **得** — degree complement: 高兴得想哭 (so happy that...)

sentences.base_language: My whole family is very proud of me.
sentences.target_language: 我全家人都为我感到骄傲。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ quán jiārén dōu wèi wǒ gǎndào jiāo'ào.
sentences.grammarNote:
**都** (dōu) = 'all / both'. It requires a plural or collective subject: **全家人都** (the whole family all…). **都** always comes before the verb.
**为** here = 'for / on behalf of': **为你高兴** (happy for you), **为我感到骄傲** (feel proud of me).
**骄傲** (jiāo'ào) = proud. Note: can also mean 'arrogant' when used negatively — context determines meaning.

**应该** (yīnggāi) = 'should / ought to'. Modal verbs in Chinese come before the main verb, same position as **想**, **要**, **能**, **可以**.
**一下** softens: 'celebrate a bit / have a little celebration'.
**庆祝** (qìngzhù) = to celebrate — **庆** = to celebrate/rejoice, **祝** = to wish/bless.

sentences.base_language: Let's go to dinner to celebrate.
sentences.romanized: Wǒmen qù chī dùn fàn qìngzhù ba.
sentences.grammarNote:
**顿** (dùn) is the measure word for meals: **一顿饭** (a meal). **吃顿饭** = 'eat a meal'. The purpose clause **庆祝** (celebrate) comes after the main action, unlike English where it might come first.
**吧** (ba) at the end of a sentence: turns it into a suggestion or soft proposal: **去吧** = 'let's go', **对吧** = 'right?'

sentences.base_language: This is the best day of my life.
sentences.target_language: 这是我人生中最美好的一天。
sentences.romanized: Zhè shì wǒ rénshēng zhōng zuì měihǎo de yītiān.
sentences.grammarNote:
**最** (zuì) = most — superlative marker, placed before the adjective: **最好** (best), **最美** (most beautiful), **最快乐** (happiest).
**人生** (rénshēng) = one's life / human life (**人** = person, **生** = life/birth). More philosophical than **生活** (shēnghuó = daily life).
**美好** (měihǎo) = wonderful/beautiful (of life, memories, times). **美** = beautiful, **好** = good.

sentences.base_language: I worked really hard for this.
sentences.target_language: 我为这件事付出了很多努力。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ wèi zhè jiàn shì fùchū le hěn duō nǔlì.
sentences.grammarNote:
**件** (jiàn) is the measure word for matters, affairs, clothing items: **一件事** (a matter), **一件衬衫** (a shirt).
**付出** = 'to put in (effort/sacrifice)' — **付** = pay, **出** = out: to pay out effort.
**努力** (nǔlì) = effort / to work hard — works as both noun and verb: **很多努力** (a lot of effort), **我很努力** (I work very hard).
The **了** here marks the completed effort.
